clinicalStem: A 22-year-old female university student presents with a 6-month history of episodic chest tightness, wheeze, and dry cough — predominantly at night and after exercise. She is otherwise healthy, has a family history of eczema, and keeps two cats at home. On examination between episodes her chest is clear. Spirometry shows FEV1 72% predicted, FVC 88% predicted, FEV1/FVC ratio 0.78. Which single investigation result would BEST confirm the diagnosis?
options: Post-bronchodilator FEV1 improvement of ≥12% AND ≥200 mL
Peak expiratory flow variability of <10% over two weeks
Positive methacholine challenge with PC20 >16 mg/mL
Serum total IgE level above 150 IU/mL
High-resolution CT thorax showing air trapping
explanation: A post-bronchodilator FEV1 increase of ≥12% AND ≥200 mL from baseline demonstrates significant reversible airflow obstruction — the hallmark spirometric criterion for asthma diagnosis per GINA 2024. Both thresholds must be met to distinguish true reversibility from measurement variability. Her clinical picture (young, atopic, nocturnal/exertional symptoms, normal exam between attacks) strongly supports asthma, and reversibility testing confirms it.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — reversibility of ≥12% AND ≥200 mL in FEV1 is the spirometric confirmation criterion for asthma per GINA 2024.
A diurnal PEF variability >10% (ideally >20%) supports asthma; variability <10% would actually argue against it.
Methacholine PC20 <8 mg/mL indicates airway hyperresponsiveness; a PC20 >16 mg/mL is effectively normal and would not confirm asthma.
Elevated IgE supports an atopic state but is not diagnostic of asthma; it is non-specific and can be raised in many allergic and parasitic conditions.
HRCT is not a first-line investigation for asthma; it is used to exclude structural lung disease or complications, not to confirm the diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: For asthma, spirometric reversibility requires BOTH ≥12% AND ≥200 mL increase in FEV1 post-bronchodilator — meeting only one threshold does not confirm the diagnosis.
keyClue: Young atopic female with nocturnal/exertional wheeze and borderline spirometry — reversibility testing is the next step.
commonExamTrap: Choosing elevated IgE as diagnostic — it supports atopy but does not confirm asthma.
missedConcept: Both the percentage AND absolute volume criteria for FEV1 reversibility must be satisfied simultaneously.

clinicalStem: A 64-year-old retired builder with a 45 pack-year smoking history presents with progressive exertional dyspnoea and a productive morning cough for the past five years. He has never been diagnosed with asthma. On examination he has a barrel-shaped chest, prolonged expiratory phase, and scattered expiratory wheeze. Spirometry before bronchodilator shows FEV1 58% predicted, FVC 75% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.71. After salbutamol 400 mcg via spacer, FEV1 rises to 62% predicted (an increase of 90 mL, 6.9%). Which statement about confirming COPD in this patient is MOST accurate?
options: Post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 confirms airflow obstruction consistent with COPD
The degree of reversibility (6.9% / 90 mL) confirms asthma and excludes COPD
A bronchodilator response of any size rules out COPD
COPD requires FEV1 <50% predicted regardless of FEV1/FVC ratio
Chest X-ray showing hyperinflation is sufficient to diagnose COPD without spirometry
explanation: GOLD guidelines define COPD as a post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC ratio <0.70 in the appropriate clinical context (significant smoking history, chronic symptoms). This patient's post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC is still clearly reduced, confirming persistent (fixed) airflow obstruction. The modest bronchodilator response does not reach the asthma threshold (≥12% AND ≥200 mL), and does not exclude COPD.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 is the GOLD diagnostic criterion for COPD.
The bronchodilator response here (6.9%, 90 mL) does NOT meet the asthma criteria of ≥12% AND ≥200 mL simultaneously; partial reversibility is common in COPD.
Some bronchodilator response is seen in many COPD patients; it does not exclude the diagnosis.
GOLD staging uses FEV1 % predicted to grade severity but the FEV1/FVC ratio <0.70 is the diagnostic threshold — FEV1 alone does not define COPD.
Hyperinflation on CXR supports COPD but is neither sensitive nor specific; post-bronchodilator spirometry is the gold-standard diagnostic test.
clinicalPearl: COPD diagnosis requires post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70; clinical context (smoking, symptoms) and spirometry together make the diagnosis — not CXR alone.
keyClue: 45 pack-year smoker, fixed obstruction (FEV1/FVC <0.70) that persists after bronchodilator.
commonExamTrap: Thinking that ANY bronchodilator response means asthma — partial reversibility is common in COPD and does not exclude it.
missedConcept: Post-bronchodilator spirometry is mandatory; pre-bronchodilator values alone are insufficient to diagnose COPD.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man with a 30 pack-year smoking history and a childhood diagnosis of asthma presents with worsening breathlessness and wheeze over 18 months. He uses a salbutamol inhaler frequently. Spirometry shows FEV1 61% predicted, FVC 82% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.69. After 400 mcg salbutamol, FEV1 improves to 74% predicted — a rise of 210 mL (21%). Serum eosinophils are 0.42 × 10⁹/L. Chest X-ray shows mild hyperinflation. Which diagnosis BEST describes this patient's condition?
explanation: This patient has features of BOTH asthma (childhood onset, significant post-bronchodilator reversibility ≥12% AND ≥200 mL, blood eosinophilia) AND COPD (age >40, heavy smoking history, persistent airflow obstruction post-bronchodilator with FEV1/FVC <0.70, hyperinflation on CXR). ACOS is characterised by persistent airflow limitation with several features associated with both conditions. Current GINA/GOLD guidance uses the term to describe this overlap phenotype.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — the combination of persistent post-bronchodilator obstruction, significant reversibility, eosinophilia, smoking history, and prior asthma diagnosis meets ACOS criteria.
COPD alone does not explain the significant reversibility (≥12% AND ≥200 mL) and elevated eosinophils; the asthma history is also relevant.
ABPA presents with central bronchiectasis, mucus plugging, precipitating antibodies to Aspergillus, and markedly elevated IgE — none of which are present here.
Pure asthma does not persist with fixed post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70, and 30 pack-years of smoking is a major confounding factor against a pure asthma label at this age.
Hypersensitivity pneumonitis is a restrictive/mixed pattern disease caused by inhaled antigens; it does not cause obstructive spirometry with high reversibility.
clinicalPearl: ACOS should be suspected when a patient has BOTH persistent airflow obstruction AND substantial bronchodilator reversibility, especially with a history of asthma AND significant smoking.
keyClue: Childhood asthma + 30 pack-years + fixed obstruction + reversibility ≥12% AND ≥200 mL + eosinophilia = ACOS.
commonExamTrap: Labelling as pure COPD because of the smoking history, missing the significant reversibility and asthma features.
missedConcept: ACOS is not asthma plus smoking — it requires persistent post-bronchodilator airflow limitation alongside asthma features; both conditions genuinely coexist.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman with known asthma is brought to the emergency department unable to complete sentences. Her respiratory rate is 32/min, heart rate 118 bpm, SpO2 90% on air, and PEFR is 38% of personal best. She has used her salbutamol nebuliser six times in the last hour at home without improvement. On auscultation there is diffuse wheeze but also a silent right lower zone. Which single investigation is MOST urgently required at this point?
explanation: This patient has life-threatening asthma features (SpO2 <92%, RR >25, PEFR <33% though here 38% approaching that threshold, silent chest zone) indicating near-fatal attack. An ABG is urgently required to detect CO2 retention — a rising or normal PaCO2 in a severely distressed asthmatic signals impending respiratory failure and the need for ICU/ventilatory support. SpO2 alone cannot detect hypercapnia. The silent zone raises the concern of pneumothorax which ABG plus CXR together will help exclude, but the immediate priority is gas exchange assessment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — ABG is the most urgent investigation; a normal or raised PaCO2 in severe asthma signals impending respiratory failure requiring escalation.

CXR is important to exclude pneumothorax given the silent right lower zone, but ABG gives more immediate physiological information about the severity of respiratory failure and should not delay treatment.
Serum potassium is important (repeated salbutamol causes hypokalaemia) but is not the MOST urgent — gas exchange assessment takes priority.
PEFR has already been measured at 38%; repeating it without an ABG misses the critical question of whether CO2 retention is developing.
Formal spirometry is contraindicated in acute severe asthma — it is a time-consuming elective investigation inappropriate in this emergency context.
clinicalPearl: In acute severe asthma, a normal or rising PaCO2 is ominous — it means the exhausted patient can no longer hyperventilate to maintain CO2 clearance and indicates near-fatal attack.
keyClue: Severe asthma + SpO2 90% + silent chest zone + failure to respond to repeated nebulisers → ABG to detect CO2 retention.
commonExamTrap: Ordering CXR first — while important to exclude pneumothorax, ABG provides the most critical immediate information about impending respiratory failure.
missedConcept: In asthma, hypercapnia (not just hypoxia) signals the need for ICU; ABG is the only way to detect it.
redFlagReason: Life-threatening/near-fatal asthma: SpO2 <92%, silent chest, failure of repeated bronchodilators — CO2 retention on ABG indicates impending respiratory arrest requiring immediate ICU escalation.

clinicalStem: A 67-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with a 3-day history of productive cough with rusty-coloured sputum, fever of 38.9°C, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. On examination RR is 24/min, BP 108/72 mmHg, heart rate 96 bpm, SpO2 93% on air. Chest auscultation reveals bronchial breathing and dullness to percussion at the right base. Blood urea is 9.8 mmol/L. Which initial investigation will BEST confirm the diagnosis AND guide early management decisions?
explanation: Chest X-ray is the gold-standard initial investigation to confirm community-acquired pneumonia — it demonstrates lobar or segmental consolidation, guides severity assessment (bilateral vs unilateral, extent of consolidation), excludes complications (effusion, abscess), and, together with clinical scoring (CURB-65), determines inpatient vs outpatient management. This patient has a CURB-65 score of 3 (Urea >7, RR ≥30 approaching, BP <90 systolic or <60 diastolic — BP is borderline), indicating severe CAP requiring hospital admission.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — CXR confirms consolidation, establishes severity, and directs management; it is the first-line investigation for suspected CAP.
CTPA is indicated for suspected pulmonary embolism; while PE can mimic pneumonia, it is not the first investigation here — CXR comes first.
Sputum culture is important for microbiological identification but frequently negative and results are delayed; it does not confirm the diagnosis or guide immediate management decisions.
Bronchoscopy is invasive and reserved for immunocompromised patients, non-resolving pneumonia, or when malignancy is suspected — not for straightforward CAP.
Pleural ultrasound is valuable if effusion is suspected for characterisation and drainage guidance, but does not confirm consolidation and is not the primary diagnostic tool.
clinicalPearl: CXR + CURB-65 scoring together determine diagnosis AND admission decision in CAP; CURB-65 ≥3 indicates severe CAP requiring hospital care with consideration of ICU.
keyClue: Rusty sputum + bronchial breathing + dullness to percussion + elevated urea = CAP; CXR confirms and CURB-65 guides severity.
commonExamTrap: Requesting CTPA first to 'exclude PE' — clinical features here are far more consistent with pneumonia; CXR is the appropriate first investigation.
missedConcept: CURB-65 requires knowing the CXR result (bilateral vs unilateral) in addition to clinical variables to fully stratify severity.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old alcoholic man presents with a 3-week history of swinging fevers, night sweats, foul-smelling purulent sputum, and weight loss of 5 kg. He has poor dentition. On examination he is febrile at 39.2°C, clubbing is noted, and there is dullness with bronchial breathing over the right lower zone posteriorly. CXR shows a round opacity in the right lower lobe with an air-fluid level. Which investigation finding would BEST distinguish lung abscess from a cavitating carcinoma in this patient?
options: CT thorax showing a thick irregular wall and hilar lymphadenopathy
CT thorax showing a smooth thin-walled cavity with surrounding consolidation
Sputum cytology positive for malignant cells
explanation: A lung abscess classically appears on CT as a thick-walled cavity with a smooth inner margin, surrounded by consolidation/pneumonitis, often with an air-fluid level — consistent with liquefactive necrosis from infection. In contrast, a cavitating bronchogenic carcinoma typically shows a thick irregular wall (>15 mm), nodular inner margin, and associated lymphadenopathy or satellite lesions. The smooth thin-walled appearance with surrounding consolidation strongly favours abscess over malignancy in the correct clinical context.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Thick irregular wall and hilar lymphadenopathy are features of cavitating malignancy — this finding would point AWAY from lung abscess, not confirm it.
Correct — smooth thin-walled cavity with surrounding consolidation (and air-fluid level) on CT is characteristic of lung abscess in this clinical context.
Positive sputum cytology would confirm malignancy rather than abscess — useful if carcinoma is suspected but not the finding that distinguishes abscess.
Endobronchial tumour on bronchoscopy confirms carcinoma, not abscess; it is a finding of malignancy.
Serum CEA is a tumour marker; elevation supports malignancy but is non-specific and would not confirm abscess over carcinoma.
clinicalPearl: Lung abscess: smooth thin-walled cavity with air-fluid level in a consolidation background. Cavitating carcinoma: thick (>15 mm) irregular nodular wall ± lymphadenopathy — CT distinguishes the two.
keyClue: Alcoholic + poor dentition + aspiration risk + foul sputum + air-fluid level = lung abscess until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Ordering sputum cytology as the distinguishing test — it would identify malignancy but CT morphology of the cavity wall is the primary radiological differentiator.
missedConcept: Wall thickness and internal margin regularity on CT are the key radiological features distinguishing abscess from cavitating carcinoma.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old male university student presents with a 10-day history of gradual-onset dry cough, low-grade fever, headache, and malaise. His roommate had a similar illness 3 weeks ago. He has a macular rash on his trunk. SpO2 is 96% on air. CXR shows bilateral patchy interstitial infiltrates disproportionately severe compared to his mild clinical appearance. Auscultation reveals minimal fine crackles bilaterally. Which investigation would BEST confirm the most likely causative organism?
options: Serology for Mycoplasma pneumoniae IgM (and paired acute/convalescent titres)
Sputum Gram stain showing Gram-positive diplococci
Nasopharyngeal PCR for influenza A and B
explanation: The clinical triad of gradual-onset dry cough in a young person, CXR changes worse than clinical signs ('walking pneumonia'), and a maculopapular rash (erythema multiforme-like) in a cluster outbreak strongly points to Mycoplasma pneumoniae atypical pneumonia. Mycoplasma cannot be cultured on standard media; serological detection of IgM antibodies (positive by day 7-10) or a four-fold rise in paired titres is the standard diagnostic confirmation method.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — Mycoplasma IgM serology (and paired titres) is the best confirmatory investigation for Mycoplasma pneumoniae, which cannot be grown on standard culture media.
Gram stain for diplococci would identify Streptococcus pneumoniae (typical pneumonia); Mycoplasma is a wall-less organism and does not appear on Gram stain.
Blood cultures for S. pneumoniae would miss Mycoplasma; the clinical picture (gradual onset, dry cough, rash, interstitial pattern) is not typical bacterial pneumonia.
Urinary pneumococcal antigen detects S. pneumoniae; it has no role in the diagnosis of Mycoplasma.
Influenza PCR is appropriate if influenza is suspected; the 10-day prodrome, cluster involvement, and rash are more characteristic of Mycoplasma than influenza.
clinicalPearl: Mycoplasma pneumoniae: young adult, dry cough, 'worse X-ray than patient' (interstitial infiltrates), rash, and cluster outbreak — serology (IgM) is diagnostic as it does not grow on standard culture.
keyClue: Bilateral interstitial infiltrates worse than clinical signs + skin rash + young patient + cluster outbreak = Mycoplasma pneumoniae.
commonExamTrap: Ordering sputum Gram stain — Mycoplasma has no cell wall and is invisible on Gram stain; it requires serology.
missedConcept: Atypical pathogens (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella) cannot be identified by standard Gram stain/culture — specific serological or molecular testing is required.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old Sudanese refugee presents to a UK chest clinic with a 3-month history of productive cough, haemoptysis, drenching night sweats, and 8 kg unintentional weight loss. He smokes 10 cigarettes per day and has no prior TB history. Temperature is 37.8°C. CXR shows right upper lobe heterogeneous consolidation with early cavitation. Sputum smear microscopy (Ziehl-Neelsen stain) is positive for acid-fast bacilli. Which additional investigation is MOST important to perform BEFORE starting anti-TB therapy?
options: Sputum mycobacterial culture with drug susceptibility testing (DST)
Bronchoscopy with BAL for TB PCR
CT thorax to characterise cavitation extent
explanation: Sputum culture with drug susceptibility testing (DST) is mandatory before starting anti-TB therapy. AFB smear confirms acid-fast bacilli but cannot differentiate Mycobacterium tuberculosis from non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM), nor does it detect drug resistance. DST identifies resistance to rifampicin, isoniazid, and second-line drugs — critical information given rising rates of MDR-TB, especially in refugees from high-burden countries. Starting standard HRZE empirically without knowing sensitivity patterns risks treatment failure in MDR-TB.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — sputum culture with DST is essential before treatment; it confirms M. tuberculosis species identity and detects drug resistance, particularly MDR-TB.
Bronchoscopy with BAL-PCR is useful when sputum cannot be obtained or is smear-negative; here sputum is smear-positive so bronchoscopy is not the priority.
CT thorax is useful for characterising disease extent and complications but does not influence the choice of anti-TB drugs; DST does.
HIV serology is strongly recommended for all TB patients (co-infection is common and affects management) but is not the investigation needed BEFORE starting anti-TB therapy specifically.
TST/Mantoux has no role in active TB management — it cannot distinguish latent from active disease and results are often false-negative in active disease due to anergy.
clinicalPearl: Always obtain mycobacterial culture with DST before starting TB treatment — smear positivity confirms AFBs but only culture + DST identifies the species and resistance profile.
keyClue: AFB-positive smear + refugee from high-burden country + cavitation = risk of MDR-TB; DST must be done before treatment.
commonExamTrap: Starting empirical HRZE without DST — in MDR-TB this constitutes effective monotherapy and will select for further resistance.
missedConcept: AFB smear cannot differentiate M. tuberculosis from NTM, and cannot detect drug resistance — only culture with DST can do both.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old healthcare worker originally from Pakistan, now working on a respiratory ward in the UK, is referred for latent TB infection (LTBI) screening after a significant contact exposure to a smear-positive index case. He received BCG vaccination as a child (documented scar). He has no symptoms, normal CXR, and no prior TB diagnosis. Which investigation is the MOST appropriate first-line test for LTBI in this individual?
options: Tuberculin skin test (Mantoux) with 2 TU PPD
explanation: IGRA (e.g., QuantiFERON-TB Gold or T-SPOT.TB) is the preferred LTBI test in BCG-vaccinated adults in the UK per NICE guidelines. Unlike the TST (Mantoux), IGRA uses TB-specific antigens (ESAT-6, CFP-10) absent from BCG and most NTM, so it does not produce false-positive results due to BCG vaccination — a critical advantage in a BCG-vaccinated healthcare worker. NICE guidance recommends IGRA as first-line in all adults ≥18 years for LTBI testing.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — IGRA is recommended as the preferred LTBI test in BCG-vaccinated individuals because it is not affected by prior BCG vaccination, unlike the Mantoux test.

TST/Mantoux is subject to false-positive results from prior BCG vaccination; in a documented BCG-vaccinated adult, a positive Mantoux cannot reliably distinguish vaccine response from LTBI — IGRA is superior.
Sputum smear microscopy is a test for active TB; this patient has no symptoms and a normal CXR — active TB is not suspected, and smear has no role in LTBI screening.
HRCT is not indicated for LTBI screening in an asymptomatic individual with a normal CXR; it would expose the patient to unnecessary radiation.
Serial CXR monitoring has no role in LTBI screening — CXR cannot detect LTBI and serial imaging is not a diagnostic strategy.
clinicalPearl: In BCG-vaccinated individuals, IGRA is preferred over TST for LTBI testing because BCG vaccination can cause false-positive TST reactions but does NOT affect IGRA specificity.
keyClue: BCG-vaccinated healthcare worker + significant TB contact exposure + screening for LTBI = IGRA is the first-line test.
commonExamTrap: Choosing Mantoux (TST) in a BCG-vaccinated person — the high false-positive rate from BCG makes it unreliable in this context.
missedConcept: IGRA targets ESAT-6 and CFP-10, antigens specific to M. tuberculosis complex and absent from BCG strains — this is why BCG does not cause false-positive IGRA results.

clinicalStem: A 27-year-old Eritrean asylum seeker presents with 6 weeks of right-sided pleuritic chest pain, progressive dyspnoea, and constitutional symptoms (night sweats, 7 kg weight loss). CXR shows a moderate right-sided pleural effusion. Diagnostic thoracocentesis yields straw-coloured exudative fluid: protein 55 g/L, LDH 380 U/L, glucose 3.1 mmol/L, pH 7.22, lymphocyte predominance 92%, no malignant cells on cytology. AFB smear of pleural fluid is negative. Which investigation has the HIGHEST diagnostic yield for confirming tuberculous pleuritis?
options: Pleural biopsy (closed or thoracoscopic) with histology and culture
Pleural fluid ADA (adenosine deaminase) level alone
Pleural fluid PCR for M. tuberculosis
explanation: Pleural biopsy — either Abrams closed needle biopsy or thoracoscopic biopsy — with histology showing caseating granulomas AND culture has the highest combined diagnostic yield (60-80%) for TB pleuritis. Pleural fluid smear sensitivity is very low (<10%); fluid culture is also relatively low yield (~30-40%). Biopsy histology demonstrating granulomatous inflammation is often diagnostic even when cultures are negative. Thoracoscopic biopsy yields the highest sensitivity of all methods.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — pleural biopsy with histology (granulomas) and culture provides the highest diagnostic yield for TB pleuritis, significantly superior to fluid analysis alone.

Pleural fluid ADA >40 IU/L supports TB pleuritis diagnosis and is useful when biopsy is unavailable, but it is not specific (can be raised in empyema, malignancy, rheumatoid) and has lower specificity as a standalone test; it does not confirm the diagnosis.
Sputum AFB smear/culture ×3 is important for active pulmonary TB but has low yield in isolated pleural TB where pulmonary disease is minimal or absent.
Pleural fluid PCR has variable sensitivity (50-70%) and specificity and is highly dependent on laboratory technique; it is inferior to biopsy histology plus culture.
Serum IGRA confirms M. tuberculosis exposure/infection but cannot distinguish pleural TB from pulmonary or latent disease; it does not confirm the site of disease.
clinicalPearl: TB pleuritis: pleural fluid is AFB-smear negative in >90% of cases; pleural biopsy with histology (caseating granulomas) and culture is the highest-yield diagnostic test.
keyClue: Exudative lymphocytic effusion + negative smear + young person from high-burden country + constitutional symptoms → pleural biopsy for highest diagnostic yield.
commonExamTrap: Relying on ADA alone to diagnose TB pleuritis — while useful, ADA is not specific and biopsy is required for definitive diagnosis.
missedConcept: In TB pleuritis, AFB smear of pleural fluid is almost always negative; biopsy, not fluid analysis, is the cornerstone of diagnosis.

clinicalStem: A 60-year-old woman presents with progressive breathlessness and reduced exercise tolerance. On examination of the respiratory system: trachea is deviated to the LEFT; on the RIGHT side there is stony dullness to percussion, absent breath sounds, absent tactile vocal fremitus, and absent vocal resonance. The left side is normal. Which diagnosis is MOST consistent with these examination findings?
explanation: A large pleural effusion produces the classic triad: stony dullness on percussion (pathognomonic — dullest of all respiratory signs), absent breath sounds, and absent vocal fremitus/resonance (fluid transmits neither). With a massive effusion, the trachea and mediastinum deviate AWAY from the effusion side (toward the left), due to the space-occupying nature of the fluid. This combination perfectly fits a large right pleural effusion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — large pleural effusion: stony dullness, absent breath sounds, absent fremitus/resonance, trachea deviated AWAY from the affected side.
Lobar collapse causes tracheal deviation TOWARD the affected side (due to volume loss) and the dullness is not stony; bronchial breathing may be heard if the airway is patent.

Tension pneumothorax causes tracheal deviation AWAY from the affected side (as with effusion) but percussion is HYPER-resonant (not dull), and breath sounds are absent — this is a life-threatening emergency.
Consolidation produces dull percussion, but bronchial breathing and INCREASED vocal resonance/fremitus (enhanced transmission through solid lung) — not absent resonance as seen here.
Pleural thickening can cause dullness but typically does not cause the degree of mediastinal shift seen here, and breath sounds are usually reduced but not absent.
clinicalPearl: Stony dullness on percussion is pathognomonic of pleural effusion. Consolidation causes dullness too, but crucially has INCREASED (not absent) vocal fremitus and bronchial breathing.
keyClue: Stony dullness + absent breath sounds + absent vocal fremitus + trachea deviated AWAY = large pleural effusion.
commonExamTrap: Confusing the direction of tracheal deviation — it deviates TOWARD the lesion in collapse (volume loss) but AWAY from the lesion in effusion/tension pneumothorax (space-occupying).
missedConcept: Consolidation and effusion both cause dullness, but their vocal fremitus/resonance findings are OPPOSITE: increased in consolidation, absent in effusion.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old man is referred to the respiratory clinic with progressive breathlessness over 2 years. On inspection, he has grade 3 finger clubbing (obliteration of the nail-fold angle, drumstick appearance), bilateral fine end-inspiratory 'Velcro' crackles at both bases, central cyanosis, and no wheeze. He has never smoked. Spirometry shows FVC 62% predicted, FEV1 65% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.88. Which diagnosis is BEST supported by the combination of examination findings and spirometry?
explanation: The combination of progressive dyspnoea in a non-smoker, bilateral fine 'Velcro' end-inspiratory crackles at the bases, clubbing, central cyanosis, and a RESTRICTIVE spirometric pattern (reduced FVC, preserved or raised FEV1/FVC ratio — here 0.88 is above the obstruction threshold, indicating restriction) is the classic picture of IPF. IPF is characterised by usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern, and these peripheral inspiratory crackles are highly characteristic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — IPF classically presents with Velcro crackles, clubbing, restrictive spirometry (raised FEV1/FVC), and progressive dyspnoea in a non-smoker aged >50.
COPD produces an obstructive pattern (FEV1/FVC <0.70), is strongly associated with smoking history, and causes hyperinflation and wheeze — not restriction and Velcro crackles.
Bronchiectasis causes coarse wet crackles (not Velcro), productive cough, and an obstructive or mixed spirometric pattern; clubbing may occur but the crackle character is different.
Cryptogenic organising pneumonia (COP) typically causes consolidative patches on HRCT, responds to steroids, and does not usually cause significant clubbing or the chronic progressive course of IPF.

Pleural mesothelioma presents with a pleural effusion/thickening pattern, pleural pain, and restrictive physiology from reduced lung expansion — it does not cause the bilateral basal crackles characteristic of fibrosis.
clinicalPearl: Bilateral fine end-inspiratory 'Velcro' crackles + clubbing + restrictive spirometry (FEV1/FVC ≥0.70 with reduced FVC) in a non-smoker aged >50 = IPF until proven otherwise.
keyClue: Velcro crackles + clubbing + restrictive pattern (high FEV1/FVC, low FVC) + non-smoker = IPF.
commonExamTrap: Misinterpreting an FEV1/FVC of 0.88 as normal lung function — in restriction, FVC falls proportionally more than FEV1, so the RATIO is preserved or elevated despite significant disease.
missedConcept: Restrictive lung disease has a normal or HIGH FEV1/FVC ratio — the diagnostic clue is a REDUCED FVC with preserved or raised ratio, not an obstructive pattern.
